era_context: The early 15th century continues with the Council of Constance still in session (1414–1418), addressing the Great Schism and heresy. The Hussite movement is gaining momentum in Bohemia following Jan Hus's execution in 1415. The Hundred Years' War between England and France is ongoing, with English forces under Henry V making significant gains in Normandy. The Ming Dynasty is consolidating power in China, and Portuguese maritime exploration is beginning to expand into the Atlantic.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia and Steppe regions (limited European documentation)

events.title: Council of Constance: Election of Pope Martin V
events.description: On 11 November 1417, the Council of Constance elected Cardinal Oddone Colonna as Pope Martin V, ending the Great Schism that had divided the Catholic Church since 1378. Martin V's election was a major achievement of the council and restored a single, undisputed papal authority. However, the council's condemnation of Jan Hus (1415) and its assertion of conciliar authority over the papacy created tensions that would persist for centuries. Martin V's papacy (1417–1431) focused on consolidating papal power and addressing the remaining schismatic factions.
key_figures: Cardinal Oddone Colonna (elected Pope Martin V)
Sigismund of Hungary (Holy Roman Emperor, patron of the council)

sources.name: Council of Constance, official acts and decrees
Ulrich of Richenthal, Chronicle of the Council of Constance

events.certainty_note: The election of Pope Martin V on 11 November 1417 is well-documented in official council records and contemporary chronicles.

events.title: Hundred Years' War: English Conquest of Normandy
events.description: In 1417, English forces under Henry V were consolidating their conquest of Normandy, which had begun after the Battle of Agincourt (1415). The English were systematically capturing Norman towns and fortifications, establishing English control over the region. This campaign demonstrated the effectiveness of English military tactics, including the use of longbowmen and siege warfare. By 1417, most of Normandy was under English control, and Henry V was preparing to advance further into France.
events.certainty_note: The English conquest of Normandy in 1417 is well-documented in contemporary English and French chronicles.

events.title: Ming Dynasty: Forbidden City Construction
events.description: In 1417, construction of the Forbidden City in Beijing was well underway. The Yongle Emperor had commissioned this massive palace complex in 1406, and by 1417, the main structures were nearing completion. The Forbidden City represented the pinnacle of Ming architectural achievement and served as the imperial residence and seat of government. The construction involved hundreds of thousands of workers and demonstrated the Ming Dynasty's wealth, power, and organisational capabilities. The Forbidden City was completed in 1420.
key_figures: Yongle Emperor (Ming Emperor, patron of the project)
Cai Xin (chief architect, though attribution is uncertain)
events.certainty_note: The construction of the Forbidden City is well-documented in Ming records. The dates (1406–1420) are well-established.

events.title: Ottoman Empire: Consolidation under Mehmed I
events.description: In 1417, Mehmed I (r. 1413–1421) was consolidating Ottoman power in Anatolia and the Balkans. The Ottoman state was expanding its territorial control and military capabilities, setting the stage for later expansion. This period saw the development of Ottoman administrative structures and the refinement of the Janissary corps. The Ottoman Empire was becoming a major regional power, though it had not yet challenged Byzantine Constantinople directly.
events.certainty_note: The broader context of Ottoman consolidation is well-attested, but specific events in 1417 are not distinctly documented. This entry describes the era context rather than a discrete event.

events.title: Zheng He's Treasure Voyages: Third Voyage Preparation
events.description: In 1417, preparations were underway for Zheng He's third treasure voyage (1409–1411 had been the second voyage). The Ming Dynasty was continuing its policy of maritime exploration and diplomatic expansion under the Yongle Emperor. These voyages represented the peak of Ming maritime power and demonstrated advanced shipbuilding, navigation, and diplomatic capabilities. The voyages were eventually discontinued after Zheng He's death and the shift in Ming policy away from maritime expansion.

sources.name: Ma Huan, Ying-yai Sheng-lan (Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores)

events.certainty_note: The dates of Zheng He's voyages are somewhat uncertain. The third voyage is traditionally dated to 1409–1411, so 1417 would fall between voyages or during preparation for the fourth voyage (1413–1415).

disconfirming_evidence: The election of Pope Martin V is sometimes portrayed as a complete resolution of the Great Schism, but schismatic factions persisted for years after 1417. The English conquest of Normandy is sometimes presented as a straightforward military victory, but it involved complex negotiations, local resistance, and significant civilian suffering. The Forbidden City's construction is sometimes attributed to a single architect, but it was a collaborative effort involving many craftsmen and officials.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1417 is moderate in quality. European events (Council of Constance, Hundred Years' War) are well-documented from multiple perspectives. Chinese records are detailed but reflect imperial priorities. Ottoman records are available but less detailed in European archives. Sub-Saharan African and American events are largely absent from surviving written records. The Council of Constance records are particularly rich, as they were official proceedings with multiple contemporary chroniclers. The Forbidden City construction is well-documented in Ming administrative records.

graph_edges.note: The election of Pope Martin V in 1417 led to the conclusion of the Council of Constance in 1418
The execution of Jan Hus in 1415 occurred during the Council of Constance, which elected Pope Martin V in 1417
The English conquest of Normandy in 1417 led to the siege of Rouen in 1418
